summary: The narrowest stable slice was to build on the existing secret-input registry rather than add new parser/checker syntax. Emitting declaration metadata plus injector factories covered the task without destabilizing the compiler pipeline.
ergonomics: The existing secret-consumer/runtime surface was already well-factored, so adding `fromEnvironment`/`fromProvider` and module-level injector exports stayed local to the JS emitter and Bun runtime.
`test/Main.hs` already had good end-to-end secret runtime scaffolding, which made it straightforward to extend coverage without inventing a new fixture shape.
The emitted-JS string builder is still brittle: small API changes require editing many quoted lines and it is easy to introduce ordering or freeze/mutation mistakes.
follow_ups: Add first-class source syntax for secret declarations if the roadmap expects declarations to exist independently from policy permission strings.
Support explicit environment-key aliases or provider-specific metadata on secret declarations instead of assuming `environmentKey == secret name`.
Consider moving larger emitted runtime helper blocks into reusable templates/modules to reduce stringly-typed emitter edits.
warnings: Do not freeze an exported injector object before all declaration-backed fields are attached; I had to avoid mutating a frozen export.

`createProviderRuntime` now accepts `secretEnvironment` and `secretProvider`, but older `secrets` object/function behavior is still in play, so tests should cover whichever path a future change touches.
The end-to-end secret runtime assertions depend on exact JSON string ordering in `test/Main.hs`; update expected payloads carefully when adding fields.
residual_risks: Secret declarations are still derived from policy-granted secret names; there is not yet a separate source-language `secret` declaration syntax.
Environment injection currently maps each declaration to an environment key equal to the declared secret name, so alternate env-key aliases would need a follow-up change.
